schools.summary: The charge not to fear the stronger foe, for the LORD is with them: 'when thou goest out to battle against thine enemies, and seest horses, and chariots, and a people more than thou, be not afraid of them: for the LORD thy God is with thee, which brought thee up out of the land of Egypt' — the courage of faith — 'be not afraid of them: for the LORD thy God is with thee, which brought thee up out of the land of Egypt': however strong the foe, Israel was not to fear, for God who redeemed them from Egypt was with them (the witnesses noting the significance — faith in God's presence overcomes the fear of superior force, the horses, chariots, and greater numbers of the enemy not to be feared, because God is with his people, the lesson — that the presence of God is the ground of courage, not the strength of arms, the same God who delivered them from Egypt now with them in battle, the remembrance of past deliverance the ground of present faith, the truth that God's people need not fear the strongest foe, for one with God is a majority, 'if God be for us, who can be against us?' Romans 8:31, 'some trust in chariots, and some in horses: but we will remember the name of the LORD our God,' Psalm 20:7, the figure — the courage of faith that rests on God's presence, 'fear thou not; for I am with thee,' Isaiah 41:10, the truth that the battle is the LORD's, the victory not by might nor by power but by his Spirit, 'the LORD saveth not with sword and spear: for the battle is the LORD's,' 1 Samuel 17:47, the figure of the believer who faces the world, the flesh, and the devil unafraid because God is with him, the warning against the fear that looks to the enemy's strength rather than to God, the figure of the courage of faith in God's presence against the stronger foe); the charge not to fear the stronger foe, the horses and chariots no ground of fear (the courage of faith, Psalm 20:7) — the charge (be not afraid); 'for the LORD thy God is with thee, which brought thee up out of the land of Egypt' (the ground of courage, God's presence with his people, the Redeemer from Egypt with them in battle, the past deliverance the ground of faith, Romans 8:31; Isaiah 41:10) — the figure (the battle the LORD's, the victory by his presence, the providence, the charge, the priest encouraging the people to follow, vv.2-4) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: Why is Israel not to fear the stronger foe?

sides.position: Because the LORD their God is with them - faith in God's presence overcomes the fear of superior force, the horses and chariots no ground of fear because the Redeemer from Egypt is with his people (Psalm 20:7; Romans 8:31), the battle the LORD's and the victory by his presence, not by arms (1 Samuel 17:47; Isaiah 41:10)

themes: When thou goest out to battle against thine enemies, and seest horses, and chariots, and a people more than thou, be not afraid of them: for the LORD thy God is with thee, which brought thee up out of the land of Egypt
the charge not to fear the stronger foe, for the LORD is with them (the crux of faith in God's presence against superior force)
the courage that rests not on arms but on the presence of God, the enemy's horses and chariots no ground of fear because the Redeemer is with his people
the battle the LORD's and the victory by his presence (Psalm 20:7; Romans 8:31; 1 Samuel 17:47)

schools.summary: The priest's charge not to faint or fear before the enemy: 'and shall say unto them, Hear, O Israel, ye approach this day unto battle against your enemies: let not your hearts faint, fear not, and do not tremble, neither be ye terrified because of them' — the priest charges the people not to faint or fear before the enemy (the Hear, O Israel, ye approach this day unto battle against your enemies, the call to attend, the let not your hearts faint, fear not, and do not tremble, neither be ye terrified because of them, the fourfold charge against fear, the figure, the fourfold encouragement against fear, the heart not to faint before the foe, the truth that fear and faintness of heart are the soldier's enemy, faith the strength, the figure of the courage that faith gives, the heart steadied in God, the charge against the fear that the sight of the enemy breeds, the people emboldened, the encouragement to faith before the battle, vv.4); the call to attend to the charge (the address) — the call (Hear, O Israel); 'let not your hearts faint, fear not, and do not tremble, neither be ye terrified' (the fourfold charge against fear, the courage of faith, the figure, the heart steadied in God, the providence, the charge, the ground that God fights for them to follow, vv.4) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The command to offer peace to a city before attacking it: 'when thou comest nigh unto a city to fight against it, then proclaim peace unto it' — the offer of peace — 'when thou comest nigh unto a city to fight against it, then proclaim peace unto it': before besieging a city, terms of peace were first to be offered (the witnesses noting the significance — the priority of peace, even in war the offer of mercy first, the city not to be attacked until peace had been proclaimed and refused, the chance of submission without bloodshed, the lesson — that even in lawful war, mercy and peace were to be sought before the sword, the offer of peace the first resort, war the last, the humanity of the law that spared bloodshed where it could, the truth that God's people were to be peaceable even in war, seeking peace first, 'if it be possible, as much as lieth in you, live peaceably with all men,' Romans 12:18, the figure — the gospel pattern of peace offered before judgment, mercy held out before the sword falls, 'first... desireth conditions of peace,' Luke 14:32, the truth that God himself offers peace and reconciliation before judgment, the long-suffering that calls to submission, 'we pray you in Christ's stead, be ye reconciled to God,' 2 Corinthians 5:20, the figure of the offer of peace that precedes the judgment, the mercy that goes before wrath, the warning that to refuse the offered peace is to bring the war on oneself, the figure of the priority of peace and mercy, the offer made before the sword); before besieging a distant city, the offer was to be made (the war restrained by mercy) — the approach (nigh the city); 'then proclaim peace unto it' (the offer of peace before the attack, mercy and submission sought before bloodshed, the priority of peace, Romans 12:18; Luke 14:32) — the figure (the offer of peace before judgment, the gospel pattern, the providence, the offer, the terms if it accept or refuse to follow, vv.11-12) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

sides.position: The priority of peace and mercy even in war - terms of peace to be offered before the sword, the city given the chance of submission without bloodshed, the humanity of the law (Romans 12:18), a figure of the gospel offer of peace and reconciliation that precedes judgment, mercy held out before wrath (Luke 14:32; 2 Corinthians 5:20)

the protection of God's people from the snare of false worship

schools.summary: The law forbidding the destruction of fruit trees in a siege: 'when thou shalt besiege a city a long time, in making war against it to take it, thou shalt not destroy the trees thereof by forcing an axe against them: for thou mayest eat of them, and thou shalt not cut them down (for the tree of the field is man's life) to employ them in the siege' — the sparing of the trees — 'thou shalt not destroy the trees thereof by forcing an axe against them: for thou mayest eat of them... for the tree of the field is man's life': the fruit trees were not to be cut down in the siege, for they gave food and sustained life (the witnesses noting the significance — the restraint of war and the care for the creation and for life, even in war, wanton destruction was forbidden, the fruit trees that fed man to be spared, the lesson — that the law forbade needless waste and destruction, even of an enemy's resources, the fruit tree, man's sustenance, not to be destroyed in the fury of war, the distinction between the trees for food, spared, and others, used for the siege, vv.20, the truth that God's law restrains the cruelty and waste of war, sparing what serves life, 'the earth is the LORD's, and the fulness thereof,' Psalm 24:1, the figure — the broader principle of mercy and stewardship, the care for God's creation and the resources that sustain life, against the wanton destruction of war, the truth that even an enemy's fruit trees, as God's gift for the life of man, were to be spared, 'a righteous man regardeth the life of his beast,' Proverbs 12:10, the figure of the restraint that war must observe, not destroying what God gave for life, the warning against the cruelty and waste that war breeds, the law sparing the tree that feeds, the figure of the restraint of war and the care for life and the creation); the fruit trees not to be cut down in the siege, the wanton destruction forbidden (the restraint of war, Psalm 24:1) — the law (spare the trees); 'for thou mayest eat of them, and thou shalt not cut them down (for the tree of the field is man's life) to employ them in the siege' (the fruit trees spared as man's food and life, the care for the creation and what sustains life, the stewardship, Proverbs 12:10) — the figure (the restraint of war, the care for God's creation, the providence, the law, the trees not for food used in the siege to follow, vv.20) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: Why were the fruit trees not to be destroyed in a siege?

sides.position: Because the tree of the field is man's life - even in war wanton destruction is forbidden, the fruit trees that feed man to be spared, the law restraining the cruelty and waste of war and sparing what serves life (Psalm 24:1), a figure of the broader mercy and stewardship that cares for God's creation and the resources that sustain life (Proverbs 12:10)

themes: When thou shalt besiege a city a long time, in making war against it to take it, thou shalt not destroy the trees thereof by forcing an axe against them: for thou mayest eat of them, and thou shalt not cut them down (for the tree of the field is man's life) to employ them in the siege
the law forbidding the destruction of fruit trees in a siege (the crux of the restraint of war and the care for life and the creation)
even in war wanton destruction forbidden, the fruit tree that fed man spared as man's life, the law restraining the cruelty and waste of war
a figure of the mercy and stewardship that cares for God's creation and what sustains life (Psalm 24:1; Proverbs 12:10)

schools.summary: That only the non-fruit trees might be used for the siege: 'only the trees which thou knowest that they be not trees for meat, thou shalt destroy and cut them down; and thou shalt build bulwarks against the city that maketh war with thee, until it be subdued' — only trees that bore no food might be cut for the siege-works (the only the trees which thou knowest that they be not trees for meat, the non-fruit trees, the thou shalt destroy and cut them down, these might be used, the and thou shalt build bulwarks against the city that maketh war with thee, until it be subdued, the siege-works, the figure, the distinction between fruit trees, spared, and others, used for the siege, the truth that the law allowed the use of non-fruit trees, sparing only those that fed, the figure of the measured use of resources, the fruit spared, the rest employed, the siege pressed with what served no food, the city subdued, the balance of necessity and mercy, vv.19); the non-fruit trees used for the siege (the distinction) — the distinction (not for meat); 'build bulwarks against the city... until it be subdued' (the siege-works built with the non-fruit trees, the city subdued, the figure, the measured use of resources, the providence, the siege, the expiation of an uncertain murder to follow, ch.21) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Only the trees which thou knowest that they be not trees for meat, thou shalt destroy and cut them down; and thou shalt build bulwarks against the city that maketh war with thee, until it be subdued
that only the non-fruit trees might be used for the siege
only the non-fruit trees cut for the siege-works, the fruit spared and the rest employed
the balance of necessity and mercy
